1: shutu bechutszot Yerushalaim ... im timtze'u ish ... oved mishpat mevaqesh emunat (šûṭû bĕḥûṣôt yĕrûšālaim ... ʾim timṣĕʾû ʾîš ... ʿōśeh mišpāṭ mĕbaqqēš ʾĕmûnāh): 'Go through the streets of Jerusalem ... if you can find even one who acts justly and seeks faithfulness.' The standard echoes Sodom's renegotiation (Gen 18:23-32): Abraham bargained down to ten righteous people; Jeremiah is told one would suffice for Jerusalem's forgiveness. Emunat (faithfulness/truth, from aman) is the covenant attribute — the same root as amen — expressing reliability and covenant fidelity. The failure to find even one faithful person is not about sinless perfection but about covenantal orientation: there is no one whose life is fundamentally directed toward YHWH's justice.
2: veim YHWH chai yishave'u lachen lashaqer yishave'u (wĕʾim Yhwh ḥay yiššābēʿû lākēn lašāqer yiššābēʿû): 'Even when they say, 'As the LORD lives,' they are swearing falsely.' The oath 'as the LORD lives' (chai YHWH) is the highest Jewish oath formula (1 Sam 20:3; Jer 4:2). To invoke YHWH's name as surety for a lie is both perjury and a violation of the third commandment (Exod 20:7). The word shaqer (falsehood/lie) becomes one of Jeremiah's signature diagnostic terms — appearing over 30 times in the book for prophets, priests, scribes, and the entire nation.
3: YHWH eineicha halo le'emunat (Yhwh ʿênêkā hălōʾ lĕʾĕmûnāh): 'LORD, do not your eyes look for faithfulness?' The contrast between YHWH's eyes (eineicha) seeking faithfulness and the people's refusal to return structures the verse. Three responses to divine discipline are listed and rejected: they felt nothing, they refused to be corrected, they set their faces harder than stone (kashu paneihem misela). The stone-face metaphor is inverted in Ezek 36:26 and Zech 7:12: Israel's stone-heart/stone-face problem demands the divine surgery of a new heart.
4: ach ani amarti ach dalim hem niva'alu (ʾak ʾănî ʾāmartî ʾak-dallîm hēm nôʾālû): 'Then I thought, these are only the poor; they are ignorant.' Dal (poor/low/weak) often implies not just economic poverty but social vulnerability and limited access to education. Jeremiah's initial self-correction — perhaps the poor don't know better — shows genuine pastoral concern and intellectual honesty. The 'way of the LORD' (derekh YHWH) and 'the requirements of their God' (mishpat eloheihem) are what the uneducated might genuinely not know. The assumption is immediately tested and proven false in v. 5.
5: elchah li el-hagdolim vadaveram (ʾēlĕkāh-lî ʾel-haggĕdōlîm wăʾădabbĕrāh ʾôtām): 'So I went to the leaders and spoke to them.' The social experiment — test the uneducated poor, then the educated elite — proves that the moral failure is universal, not class-specific. Yachdav shaveru ol natqu moseirot (they too had together broken the yoke and torn off the bonds): the metaphor of the ol (yoke) for the covenant comes from Jeremiah's own acted parable in ch. 27-28. 'Torn off the bonds' (natqu moseirot) echoes Ps 2:3 where the nations conspire to 'burst their bonds asunder' from YHWH and his anointed.
6: aryeh miyaar hikkam ze'ev araboth yeshaddeddem (ʾaryēh miyyaʿar hikkam zĕʾēb ʿărābôt yĕšoddĕdēm): 'A lion from the forest will attack them, a wolf from the desert will ravage them, a leopard will lurk near their towns.' The three predator escalation (lion, wolf, leopard) builds in danger and ubiquity: the lion attacks directly, the wolf ravages the open fields, the leopard lurks at the city entrance — leaving no safe space. The leopard reappears in 13:23 ('can a leopard change its spots?') as an image of unreformable human nature. Ki rabbu pish'eihem atsedu meshuboteihem (their rebellions are many and their turnings-away are great): the plural meshubot (apostasies/turnings-back) has the same root as shuv (return) — their turning-away is the opposite of what Jeremiah calls them to do.
7: eich asalach lach banayich azavuni vayishavu belo-elohim (ʾêk ʾeslāḥ-lāk bānayik ʿăzābûnî wayyiššāḇĕʿû bĕlōʾ-ʾĕlōhîm): 'How can I forgive you for this? Your children have abandoned me and sworn by gods that are no gods.' The eich esalach (how can I forgive?) is a rhetorical question that takes divine forgiveness seriously — not as automatic but as requiring genuine grounds. The 'gods that are no gods' (belo-elohim) is a grammatical negation of divinity: they are called gods but have no divine reality. The adultery and flocking to brothels (bate-zonah) is the standard prophetic metaphor for idolatry (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16; Rev 17).
8: susim mezunim mashcilim hayyu ish el-eshet re'ehu (sûsîm mĕzunnānîm maśkîlîm hāyû ʾîš ʾel-ʾēšet rēʿēhû): 'They are like well-fed, lusty stallions — each neighing after another man's wife.' The horse metaphor for uncontrolled sexual desire — not merely appetite but the inability to restrain oneself — appears again at 8:6. The Ezekiel parallel (Ezek 23:20) uses the same animal imagery for Judah's political-spiritual unfaithfulness. The verse is not primarily about individual sexual ethics but about covenant unfaithfulness: the 'wife' imagery throughout the prophets frames Israel's relationship to YHWH as a marriage covenant whose violation is described in terms of adultery.
9: ha-lo al-eileh efqod neum YHWH veim begoy asher-kazeh lo titnaqem nafshi: 'Should I not call them to account for these things? ... Should I not take vengeance on a nation like this?' The rhetorical refrain at vv. 9 and 29 brackets the entire middle section of ch. 5 with the same divine question. Paqad (call to account/visit/punish) is one of Jeremiah's key theological verbs for divine intervention in history — YHWH 'visits' both in judgment and blessing (29:10: 'when I visit you ... I will fulfill my good word'). The verse assumes the answer 'yes' — accountability is a necessary feature of the moral universe.
10: alu besha'aroteiha ushachetu akhala ach kalah al ta'asu (ʿălû bĕšārôtêhā wĕšaḥātû wĕkālāh ʾal-taʿăśû): 'Advance through her vineyards and ravage them, but do not destroy them completely.' The vineyard imagery for Israel (Isa 5:1-7; Ps 80:8-16) makes the partial destruction a pruning rather than an eradication — consistent with the kalah lo eseh principle of 4:27. The branches are stripped because 'they do not belong to the LORD' — the branches that bear no covenant fruit are removed, but the vine itself (the covenant community) is not obliterated.
11: ki bagod bagdu vi beit Yisrael uveit Yehudah (kî bāgôd bāgĕdû-bî bêt-yiśrāʾēl ûbêt-yĕhûdāh): 'For the house of Israel and the house of Judah have acted completely faithlessly.' The doubled bagod bagdu (act faithlessly / act faithlessly) is the infinitive absolute construction for intensity: 'they have utterly and completely betrayed.' The naming of both houses — north (Israel) and south (Judah) — makes the indictment comprehensive: this is not a problem of one part of the covenant people but of the entire people. The word bagad (betray/act treacherously) is used of marital betrayal in Mal 2:14-16, reinforcing the marriage-covenant framework.
12: kieheshu v-YHWH vayomru lo hu (kiḥăšû bYhwh wayyōmĕrû lōʾ-hûʾ): 'They have lied about the LORD and said, 'He will do nothing.'' Kachash (lie/be deceptive/deny) is the verb for false denial — the people are not merely ignorant but actively denying that YHWH acts in history. 'He will do nothing' (lo hu, literally 'not he' — meaning YHWH is inoperative or absent from history) is the functional atheism that Jeremiah diagnoses: acknowledging YHWH's existence while denying his active governance. The consequences they deny — sword and famine — are the two classic covenant curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28.
13: vehanevi'im yihyu leruach vehaddavar ein bahem (wĕhannĕbiʾîm yihyû lĕrûaḥ wĕhaddābār ʾên bāhem): 'The prophets are nothing but wind; the word of God is not in them.' Ruach (wind/spirit/breath) is the same word for the divine Spirit. The accusation is a parody: the prophets claim to speak by the ruach (spirit) but are themselves merely ruach (empty wind). The criterion for a true prophet is the presence of the actual davar YHWH (word of God) — content that proves true over time (Deut 18:21-22). The punishment announced for the false prophets — 'what they threatened will now happen to them' — is the ironic reversal: they spoke of peace, so peace will be denied them; they denied sword and famine, so they will suffer both.
14: divaray asem befiv eish veha'am hazeh etzim va'akalam (dibārāy ʾāśem bĕpîkā ʾēš wĕhāʿam hazzeh ʿēṣîm wăʾăkālatām): 'I will make my words in your mouth a fire and these people the wood it will consume.' The fire-of-the-word image (cf. 20:9: 'his word is in my heart like a fire'; 23:29: 'is not my word like fire?') makes the prophetic word not merely informational but causally effective: the word of judgment actually brings about what it announces. YHWH Tzvaot (LORD of Armies/Hosts) is Jeremiah's favored divine title when declaring military judgment — the God of the heavenly armies marshals earthly armies as his instruments.
15: hineni mevi aleichem goi mimerachok beit Yisrael (hinnĕnî mēbîʾ ʿălêkem gôy mimmêrāḥōq bêt yiśrāʾēl): 'I am bringing a nation against you from far away, O house of Israel.' The description of Babylon at this stage (before 605 BCE) avoids naming the enemy — an 'enduring nation, an ancient nation' — which heightens the terror and matches the Deuteronomic curse of Deut 28:49 ('a nation from far away ... whose language you do not know'). The attributes: eitan (powerful/enduring, the same word for a perennial stream), meolam hu (ancient, literally 'from of old'), lo-teda leshono (language unknown to you). Unknown language = total otherness.
16: ashpato kequver patuch (ʾašpātô kĕqeber pātûaḥ): 'Their quivers are like open graves; they are all mighty men.' The open grave (qever patuch) for a quiver is a striking image of lethality: the quiver pours out arrows as a grave consumes bodies, both insatiable. Ps 5:9 applies the same 'open grave' image to the throat of the wicked (Rom 3:13 quotes it in the Pauline indictment of universal sin). The arrows from an open-grave quiver make the enemy's weapons instruments of death indistinguishable from death itself.
17: ve'achal qetzircha velahmecha ya'akhelu (wĕʾākal qĕṣîrkā wĕlaḥmekā yōʾkĕlû): 'They will devour your harvests and your food — what your sons and daughters depend on for life.' The threefold consumption — harvest, flocks and herds, vines and fig trees — dismantles the entire economic structure of covenantal blessing. In Deut 8:7-10 YHWH promised land with grain, vines, fig trees, olive oil, honey, and rich mineral resources. The nation from afar systematically reverses each of these blessings. The fortified cities they 'trust in' (batach) are a faith-object competing with YHWH — and they fail.
18: vegam bayamim hahem neum YHWH lo-eseh ittechem kalah (wĕgam bYyāmîm hāhēm nĕʾum Yhwh lōʾ-ʾeśeh ʾittkem kālāh): 'Yet even in those days, declares the LORD, I will not make a complete end of you.' The covenant restraint clause repeated from 4:27. Here it appears mid-oracle of invasion, not at the end — which makes it structurally important: YHWH states the limit of judgment even as he announces the judgment's severity. The survival of Israel through all the covenant curses is itself a theological testimony to the persistence of divine faithfulness.
19: ka'asher azavtem oti va-ta'avdu elohei nechar be'artzchem (kaʾăšer ʿăzabtûnî wattaʿabĕdû ʾĕlōhê-nēkār bĕʾarṣkem): 'As you abandoned me and served foreign gods in your own land, so now you will serve foreigners in a land that is not your own.' This is the covenant logic of measure-for-measure (middah keneged middah): the punishment mirrors the sin in form. Serving foreign gods voluntarily at home leads to serving foreigners involuntarily in a foreign land. The 'service' (avad) vocabulary is identical — the same word for worshipping gods and for slave-labor, connecting idolatry and exile as two forms of the same enslavement.
20: hagidu zot beveit Ya'akov vehashmi'uha biYehudah (haggîdû zōʾt bĕbêt yaʿăqōb wĕhaśmîʿûhā bîhûdāh): 'Announce this to the house of Jacob; proclaim it in Judah.' The messenger formula linking Jacob (the patriarchal name emphasizing the treacherous-deceiver identity, cf. 9:4) and Judah (the tribal name) frames the oracle as addressed to the whole covenant community in its historical continuity from the patriarch. Jacob's name ('he supplants/deceives') is not incidental — Jeremiah's diagnosis of the people's deceptive character will be explicit at 9:4.
21: shim'u na zot am saval velo lev (šimĕʿû-nāʾ zōʾt ʿam sāḵāl wĕʾên lēb): 'Listen to this, you foolish and senseless people — you have eyes but do not see; you have ears but do not hear.' The paradox of sighted blindness and hearing deafness is a fundamental prophetic indictment (Isa 6:9-10; Ezek 12:2). Jesus applies Isa 6:9-10 to explain why he speaks in parables (Matt 13:13-15; Mark 4:12; John 12:40). The people are not physically impaired; they have made a choice not to perceive. Am saval (foolish people) — savel is related to sivroh (refuse/burden); a people who refuse to carry the knowledge that would save them.
22: halo oti tira'u neum YHWH im mipanai lo tachilu (hălōʾ-ʾôtî tîrāʾû nĕʾum Yhwh ʾim mippānay lōʾ tāḥîlû): 'Should you not fear me? ... I am the one who set the sand as the boundary of the sea.' The argument from creation: the God who maintains the sand-boundary of the sea against its constant pressure is the same God whose law Israel is transgressing. Gvulat olam (eternal boundary/limit that it cannot cross) — olam here means 'perpetual,' established as an inviolable order. The sea's restless surging (galu yitgaashu) but inability to cross the divine boundary is the physical analogy for what Israel should be doing: they should be bounded by YHWH's law as the sea is bounded by sand. The Creator who controls the sea can certainly control them.
23: ulaam hayah la'am hazeh lev sorer umorah (ûlĕhāʿām hazzeh hāyāh lēb sôrēr ûmôreh): 'But this people has a stubborn, rebellious heart.' Two adjectives: sorer (stubborn, stiff — the root of sorrer u-moreh, 'stubborn and rebellious son' in Deut 21:18, the capital offense) and moreh (rebellious, defying authority). The contrast with v. 22's sea — the sea obeys YHWH's boundary but Israel does not — makes Israel less submissive to the Creator than inanimate creation. The heart (lev) that is the problem here will be the target of the new covenant surgery (31:33; Ezek 36:26): a new heart to replace the stubborn one.
24: velo amru belevavam nirah na et-YHWH elohenu (wĕlōʾ ʾāmĕrû bĕlibbām nîrāʾ-nāʾ ʾet-Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû): 'They do not even say in their hearts, 'Let us now fear the LORD our God.'' The seasonal rains — yoreh (early/autumn rains) and malqosh (latter/spring rains) — are the agricultural gifts that signal YHWH's continued covenant provision (Deut 11:14). The weeks of harvest (shavuot qatzir, appointed harvest weeks) are YHWH's gift, but the people do not acknowledge the giver. The failure of thanksgiving (yadah) leads directly to the failure of fear (yira): gratitude and reverence for the Creator are the two postures that covenantal life requires and that Israel has abandoned.
25: avonoteichem hitu eileh tochavot hatebah manecu otchem (ʿăwōnōtêkem hiṭṭû-ʾēlleh ûḥaṭṭōʾtêkem māneʿû haṭṭôb mikkhem): 'Your iniquities have diverted these good things, and your sins have kept them from you.' The hydraulic metaphor: sin acts as a barrier diverting the flow of covenant blessing. Hitu (diverted/turned aside) is used for water channels being turned; the imagery suggests that blessing was on its way but was deflected by the accumulation of sin. This is not arbitrary divine punishment but structural: the covenant's built-in consequence of disconnection from the source of life.
26: ki nimtze'u veammi reshi'im (kî nimṣĕʾû bĕʿammî rĕšāʿîm): 'For among my people there are wicked men — they lurk like hunters who set traps.' The predator metaphor shifts from wild animals (v. 6) to human hunters. The trap imagery (mashchit, moqesh) from Ps 91:3 and Amos 3:5 (where the trap is YHWH's) is here reversed: covenant people are trapping each other. The 'cage full of birds' in v. 27 extends the image: what hunters catch, they cage; what the wealthy gain through deception fills their houses like caged birds.
27: keclub male-of ken bateihem meluim mirma (kiklûb māleʾ ʿôp kēn bāttêhem mĕlēʾîm mirĕmāh): 'As a cage is full of birds, so their houses are full of goods gotten by deceit.' Kelub (cage/basket) is rare — only here and Amos 8:1-2 ('a basket of summer fruit'). The comparison: the ease with which birds are trapped and caged illustrates the ease with which the wealthy trap the vulnerable and fill their houses with unjust gain. Mirmah (deceit/treachery) is the Jeremianic diagnosis of structural economic injustice as fundamentally a form of relational betrayal.
28: shamnu vayishman gam avaru divrei ra (šāmĕnû wayyišmānû gam ʿābĕrû dibrê-rāʿ): 'They have grown fat and sleek; they surpass all limits in their evil deeds.' Shamen (become fat/rich) is a marker of covenant unfaithfulness in Deut 32:15: 'Jeshurun grew fat and kicked.' Wealth fattens and deadens the moral sensibility — the same critique as the 'fat heart' of Isa 6:10. The catalog of neglected justice: the orphan (yatom) whose case they do not prosecute and the needy (evyon) whose rights they do not champion are the two emblematic categories of the vulnerable (Exod 22:22; Isa 1:17; Jas 1:27).
29: The refrain of v. 9 repeated verbatim: 'Should I not call them to account for these things? ... Should I not take vengeance on a nation like this?' The structural repetition creates a courtroom rhythm: the indictment (vv. 10-28) is framed by the same divine question at its opening and close. Both times YHWH supplies his own answer implicitly: the accountability is necessary and just.
30: shama veshaavah niyetah va-aretz (šammāh wĕšaʿărûrāh niyĕtāh bāʾāreṣ): 'A horrible and shocking thing has occurred in the land.' Shama (desolation/horror, causing others to be appalled) and shaavah (shocking/appalling, producing shuddering) are paired exclamations of horror. The same word-pair appears in 18:16; 19:8; 29:18 for the reaction of bystanders to judgment. The buildup to v. 31 makes the identity of the 'horrible thing' suspenseful — the answer is not violence from without but corruption from within.
31: hanevi'im nibbe'u baseqer vehakkohanim yirdu al-yadeihem (hannĕḇîʾîm nibbĕʾû baššeqer wĕhakkōhănîm yirdû ʿal-yĕdêhem): 'The prophets prophesy what is false, the priests exercise authority on their own terms.' The phrase yirdu al-yadeihem (literally 'rule by their own hands' or 'rule at their own direction') is difficult — it may mean the priests take fees or exercise power through their own devices rather than YHWH's authority. The climax is devastating: 'my people love it this way.' The false religion is not imposed against the people's will but is exactly what they want — comfort without accountability. The rhetorical question 'but what will you do when the end comes?' (uma-ta'asu beacharitah) closes with the only answer that matters: when the reckoning comes, there is nothing to do.
